metadata.title: Build a Case Framework Selector with Claude Code: Structure vs. Frameworks

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The telecom example convinced me: profitability fits margin compression, but the fixed/variable cost split misleads because telecom cost is fixed by spectrum and regulation, so the real custom dimension is regulatory cost allocation. I want to build and pressure-test that selector. Help me: (1) sketch the Python CLI prompt that names all six standard frameworks explicitly so Claude routes rather than inventing a label; (2) design a check that the 'misleading if mechanical' output is genuinely case-specific and not a generic warning; (3) add a comparison step that runs the standard framework mechanically and shows exactly what wrong analysis it would have produced.

props.command: The telecom example convinced me: profitability fits margin compression, but the fixed/variable cost split misleads because telecom cost is fixed by spectrum and regulation, so the real custom dimension is regulatory cost allocation. I want to build and pressure-test that selector. Help me: (1) sketch the Python CLI prompt that names all six standard frameworks explicitly so Claude routes rather than inventing a label; (2) design a check that the 'misleading if mechanical' output is genuinely case-specific and not a generic warning; (3) add a comparison step that runs the standard framework mechanically and shows exactly what wrong analysis it would have produced.
props.segment: Build a Case Framework Selector with

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Applying a framework mechanically produces structures that look right but are not. The chapter warns that the candidates who score highest are not the ones who memorize the most frameworks — they are the ones who can tell when a framework does not fit and build something better on the spot. Can you make Claude surface that judgment?
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- title card: FRAMEWORK SELECTOR; the problem: mechanical application vs. fit judgment

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to build a Python CLI that takes a case prompt and outputs which standard framework is the closest natural fit, what feature of the prompt would cause that framework to mislead if applied mechanically, and one custom structural dimension specific to this problem.

props.command:
claude "Build a Python CLI case framework selector.
Input: a business case prompt.
Output: (1) which standard framework
(profitability, market entry, value chain,
pricing, M&A, growth) is the closest natural
fit and why; (2) what feature of this specific
prompt would cause that framework to mislead
if applied mechanically; (3) one custom structural
dimension not in that standard framework that is
specific to this problem.
Use the anthropic Python SDK."

beats.narration_text: Read the code before trusting it. The framework classification prompt must name the six standard frameworks explicitly so the model routes to the right one rather than inventing a new label. Verify the misleading-if-mechanical response is case-specific — a generic warning that frameworks can mislead is not what the output slot is for.

props.code:
# framework_selector.py -- Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

FRAMEWORKS = [
    "profitability", "market entry", "value chain",
    "pricing", "M&A", "growth",
]

def select(case):
    prompt = (
        f"Case: {case}\n\n"
        f"Standard frameworks: {FRAMEWORKS}\n\n"
        "Output:\n"
        "1. Closest natural fit + why.\n"
        "2. Feature of THIS prompt that would cause "
        "that framework to mislead if applied mechanically.\n"
        "3. One custom structural dimension specific "
        "to this problem not in the standard framework."
    )
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return msg.content[0].text

case = " ".join(sys.argv[1:]) or \
    "A telecom operator's EBITDA has declined 3pp despite "\
    "flat subscriber counts and stable ARPU"
print(select(case))

beats.narration_text: Run it on the telecom case: EBITDA declined three points, subscriber count flat, ARPU stable. Framework: profitability, closest fit because margin compression with flat revenue is a cost-structure problem. Misleading if mechanical: the standard framework splits costs into fixed and variable — but in telecom, the network cost structure is largely fixed by spectrum and regulation, not by volume decisions. Custom dimension: regulatory cost allocation across services.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording of terminal: profitability framework selection; mechanical limitation specific to telecom; custom regulatory-cost dimension

beats.narration_text: Add the comparison step. Run the standard framework mechanically on this case and show what analysis it generates, then compare it to what a problem-specific structure would generate. Make the gap visible.

props.command:
claude "Modify framework_selector.py:
After the three outputs, add a comparison step:
'Run the standard framework mechanically on this
case. Show what analysis it generates. Then show
what a problem-specific structure would generate.
What does the case-specific structure see that
the mechanical one misses?'"

beats.narration_text: The comparison section appears. Mechanical profitability framework generates: revenue analysis, then cost breakdown by category. Problem-specific structure generates: spectrum cost allocation by service line, network depreciation schedule versus regulatory asset life, and whether regulatory cost recovery mechanisms changed. The mechanical framework produces a correct-looking analysis of the wrong question — it looks at cost categories rather than cost-recovery logic.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: mechanical vs. problem-specific analysis side by side; the gap labeled

beats.narration_text: Frameworks are search tools, not solutions. The candidates who score highest are not the ones who have memorized the most frameworks — they are the ones who can tell when a framework does not fit and build something better on the spot. This tool makes that judgment visible.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- summary card: frameworks as search tools; fit judgment as the differentiating skill

beats.narration_text: Run two cases: one where a standard framework fits well — a straightforward profitability case with no industry-specific wrinkles — and one where mechanical application would mislead. Watch whether the custom structural dimension in the second case is genuinely novel or just a renamed framework element. If it sounds like a renamed element, the prompt engineering needs more specificity.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps card: run well-fitting vs. misleading cases; test custom dimension novelty

This tool makes that judgment visible.
